1: The move from Shittim to the Jordan's edge is the threshold moment — the last point before the crossing. Shittim was the site of Israel's greatest failure in the wilderness (Num 25, Baal Peor), so the march from Shittim to the Jordan marks the movement from failure to redemption. Christ's own path to the Jordan — from his birth in the world's Shittim (the place of failure, the cursed creation) to his baptism in the Jordan — follows the same arc. His baptism is not his own repentance but his solidarity with the sinners who need redemption. As Israel stood at the Jordan to cross into new life, Jesus stood at the Jordan to open a new humanity.
2: The three-day wait at the Jordan before the crossing — the officers going through the camp on the third day — maintains the three-day resurrection pattern that runs through the conquest narrative. Three days at the Red Sea (Exod 14-15); three days of the spies' hiding (Joshua 2:22); three days at the Jordan (Joshua 1:11, Joshua 3:2); three days at Gilgal (Joshua 5:8 — healing after circumcision). This accumulation of three-day intervals is the OT's way of marking the events that participate in YHWH's resurrection logic. Jesus identified this logic himself: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up' (John 2:19). The Jordan crossing is a resurrection event — a passing through death (the flooded river) into new life.
3: The command to follow the Ark at a distance — 'that you may know the way you shall go, for you have not passed this way before' — types Christ as the forerunner through death. 'We have this as a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul, a hope that enters into the inner place behind the curtain, where Jesus has gone as a forerunner on our behalf' (Hebrews 6:19-20). The Ark going before Israel through the Jordan types Christ going before his people through death — he has already crossed the Jordan of death and resurrection; believers follow him on the path he opened. The instruction 'you have not passed this way before' is literally true of death: no one has passed through to resurrection before Christ. He opens the way.
4: The 2,000-cubit gap between the people and the Ark — 'do not come near it' — marks the holiness-distance that the unconsecrated cannot cross. This gap is the problem that Christ resolves: 'For Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring us to God' (1 Peter 3:18). The veil of the temple, torn at Christ's death (Matthew 27:51), is the 2,000-cubit gap abolished — 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh' (Hebrews 10:19-20). The required distance between Israel and YHWH's presence was the old covenant's structure; Christ's atonement collapses it.
5: 'Sanctify yourselves, for tomorrow the LORD will do wonders among you' — the command to be holy before the miraculous crossing types baptismal preparation in the NT (1 Corinthians 6:11; Titus 3:5) and the eschatological preparation for Christ's coming: 'Beloved, we are God's children now, and what we will be has not yet appeared; but we know that when he appears we shall be like him, because we shall see him as he is. And everyone who thus hopes in him purifies himself as he is pure' (1 John 3:2-3). The 'wonders' (nifla'ot) YHWH was about to do are the same category as the cross and resurrection — the miraculous acts of divine power that change the course of history.
6: The Levitical priests carrying the Ark before the people into the Jordan types Christ bearing his cross — and with it, all humanity's sin — into death. 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24). The priests carried the Ark (YHWH's covenant presence) into the flooded river; Christ carried the covenant curse into death itself. The priests stood in the riverbed throughout the crossing — Christ remained in death for three days — until all the people had safely crossed. His 'standing in death' (the three days of burial) held open the way of salvation for all who would cross through him. Then the waters returned — the resurrection sealed off death's power.
7: 'Today I will begin to exalt you in the sight of all Israel' — YHWH's promise to exalt Joshua at the Jordan types the Father's exaltation of Christ through the resurrection: 'God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:9). The Jordan crossing was the moment of Joshua's public legitimation as Moses's true successor; the resurrection was the moment of Christ's public legitimation as the Son of God 'with power' (Romans 1:4). 'It was declared with power' — the Jordan miracle declared Joshua's authority with power; the resurrection declared Christ's authority with the same divine power. Both exaltations are YHWH's vindication of his appointed servant.
8: The priests standing in the Jordan — holding back the waters by their stationary presence with the Ark — types Christ 'standing' in death and holding open the crossing for all believers. The image of immovable priests in the flooded riverbed is a picture of Christ's faithfulness in the midst of the worst ordeal: 'He endured the cross, despising the shame' (Hebrews 12:2). The priests did not run from the flood waters; Christ did not draw back from the cup of God's wrath (Matthew 26:39-42). Their motionlessness in the Jordan was the condition of every Israelite's safe crossing; Christ's steadfast endurance of the cross is the condition of every believer's safe passage through death.
9: 'Come here and hear the words of the LORD your God' — the assembly of Israel at the Jordan to hear the divine word before crossing types every moment where Christ calls his people to hear before they act. 'My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me' (John 10:27). The Jordan crossing was not a spontaneous movement but an ordered response to heard words — the miracle followed the word, not precedes it. The NT pattern is identical: faith comes by hearing (Romans 10:17); the miracle of new birth follows the word of the gospel. Joshua assembling Israel at the Jordan to hear YHWH's word is the OT template for the gospel preacher assembling the nations before the water of baptism.
10: 'Hereby you shall know that the living God is among you' — the title 'living God' (El chai) occurs here before the Jordan miracle and is the same title Peter uses at Caesarea Philippi: 'You are the Christ, the Son of the living God' (Matthew 16:16). The Jordan miracle was YHWH's self-revelation as the living God who acts in history; the resurrection is Christ's self-revelation as the Son of the same living God. Both events prove the same thing: this is not a dead deity but the living God who defeats death. The seven Canaanite nations about to be driven out type the powers and principalities that Christ defeated through the cross (Colossians 2:15): 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame.'
11: 'Behold, the ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth is passing over before you into the Jordan' — the title 'Lord of all the earth' (adon kol ha'aretz) applied to the Ark-carrying procession types the Christ who in his death and resurrection claimed universal lordship. 'He is Lord of all' (Acts 10:36). The Ark crossing the Jordan first is YHWH claiming sovereignty over Canaan before a single Israelite foot trod the new land. Christ's resurrection is the Father's advance declaration of his Son's universal lordship before the fullness of the nations has been gathered: 'The earth is the Lord's, and the fullness thereof' (Psalms 24:1), declared and applied through Christ's cross.
12: The twelve men selected (one per tribe) to take memorial stones from the Jordan riverbed type the twelve apostles whom Christ selected to be the memorial witnesses of his resurrection. 'You will be my witnesses' (Acts 1:8) — as each tribal representative carried a stone from the miracle site, each apostle carried the testimony of the resurrection from the encounter with the risen Christ. The twelve stones at Gilgal became the visible memorial of the Jordan crossing; the twelve apostolic witnesses became the foundation of the church built on the resurrection (Ephesians 2:20). The number twelve is not accidental in either case — it is the number of covenantal completeness, the whole new Israel represented in the memorial bearers.
13: 'The waters of the Jordan shall be cut off from the waters that come down from above, and they shall stand in one heap' — the cutting off of the upper waters at Adam types Christ as the one who 'cut off' the downward flow of sin's condemnation. 'There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Romans 8:1) — the condemnation that was 'flowing down from above' (from divine judgment above into human history) is cut off by the one who bore it at the cross. The heap of water standing upstream is the accumulated wrath of God absorbed by Christ ('all your waves and billows have gone over me,' Psalms 42:7; Joshua 88:7), held back by his atoning work so that the crossing can be made safely.
14: The priests bearing the Ark step toward the Jordan 'when the people set out from their tents' — the crossing begins with corporate movement, faith expressed in community. The NT salvation event has the same communal structure: 'where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them' (Matthew 18:20). The individual cannot cross alone — the whole people move together, following the Ark. The Jordan crossing as corporate salvation act types baptism as incorporation into the body: 'For in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body' (1 Corinthians 12:13). The 'tents' they left behind type the earthly tabernacle of the body: 'We know that if the tent that is our earthly home is destroyed, we have a building from God' (2 Corinthians 5:1).
15: 'The feet of the priests bearing the Ark were dipped in the brink of the water' — the priests' first step into the flood-Jordan before the miracle preceded the miracle. This is the faith-before-sight pattern that characterizes the entire Christ-event. Thomas demanded sight before faith (John 20:25); Christ commended those who believe without seeing (John 20:29). The priest who stepped into the flooded Jordan did not see the waters part before his feet touched the water — the miracle came with the step, not before it. 'Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen' (Hebrews 11:1). The priestly foot in the Jordan is the image of faith stepping into impossible territory in reliance on YHWH's word alone.
16: The waters standing 'in a heap, very far away, at Adam' — the place-name Adam carries an unmistakable typological charge. The flooding waters of divine judgment were stopped 'at Adam' — at the point of origin of humanity's problem. Christ's atonement went all the way back to Adam: 'For as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive' (1 Corinthians 15:22). The flood held back at Adam is the curse of Adam's sin held back by Christ's atonement. The water that 'flowed down into the Sea of the Arabah (the Dead Sea)' — the waters of death flowing away to the place of death — types the removal of condemnation. 'He has taken it out of the way, nailing it to the cross' (Colossians 2:14).
17: 'And the priests bearing the Ark of the covenant of the LORD stood firm on dry ground in the midst of the Jordan, while all Israel was passing over on dry ground' — the priests standing firm while Israel crossed types Christ standing firm in death while his people pass through safely. 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21). Christ 'stood in' the Jordan of divine judgment — not for himself (he was without sin) but for all who would cross through him. His standing in the dry riverbed was neither for his own sake nor his own crossing; he stood there entirely for the sake of the people passing through. This is substitutionary atonement in its most vivid spatial image.
